steps.text: Determine whether the scope of experiments conducted at the facility were consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine whether the licensee’s procedures for the control and conduct of experiments are consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.

Determine whether the licensee reviewed and approved experiments performed at the facility, and any subsequent changes made to the experiments, in accordance with Technical Specification (TS) requirements and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Determine whether the licensee reviewed experiments for TS changes or compliance to 10 CFR 50.59.

Determine whether the licensee identified hazards associated with the experiments conducted since the last inspection and took appropriate remedial action in accordance with the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether the licensee predicted the approximate reactivity effect on the reactor before conducting the experiment.
Determine whether the licensee confirmed the reactivity effect as required by the TS.

Determine whether the licensee accounted for irradiated items until disposing of them or until they decayed to an acceptable level, in accordance with regulatory requirements and applicable licensee procedures.
Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as required by the TS.
Determine whether researchers adhered to the licensees procedural controls when loading experiments into the test facilities and removing them from the test facilities.
Determine whether the experiments reduced the operator’s ability to control reactivity manipulations or the protective system’s ability to shut down the reactor if needed.
Determine whether engineered radiation safety controls were used during experiments and posting was accomplished as required.
Examine new experiments and changes to the licensee’s experiments and related programs since the last inspection.
Review available documentation (Annual Report, experiment logs, safety committee minutes, proposals) prior to the onsite inspection.
Review the licensee's procedures for the control and conduct of experiments prior to the onsite inspection.
Review 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Compare the list of experiments to respective license conditions, TS, Safety Analysis Report (SAR), Safety Evaluation Report (SER), and supporting documentation.
Directly observe 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Review the information available to the operator that authorizes the conduct of a particular experiment to determine if it has been reviewed and approved.
Keep copies of proposals in a location specified in the licensee's procedures (usually near the reactor console).
Give particular attention to experiments that contain special nuclear material (fueled experiments) or explosives.
Conduct experiments in accordance with the licensee’s administrative control procedures.
Monitor and understand the meaning of gauges that measure pressures, including vacuum conditions, where appropriate to the experimental setup.

Verify if items such as pressure/vacuum gauges were listed in experiment proposals before the experiment was begun and if remedial actions were planned in accordance with the licensee’s administrative control procedures.
Verify that the applicable TS limits for reactivity, such as shutdown margin, excess reactivity, and individual and total worth of experiments were not exceeded.
Stop the experiment.
Calculate the value to assure compliance with constraint limits and shutdown margin limits.
Verify the location and status of three past experiments performed under the license since the last inspection.
Control irradiated articles in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 until final disposal.
Perform final disposal of irradiated articles in accordance with Subpart K of 10 CFR Part 20.
Verify that the recipient is licensed to possess the type, form, and quantity of material to be transferred.
Establish secure arrangements so that a single movement does not pulse a non-pulsing reactor, or exceed TS requirements or safety analysis assumptions.
Rest the experiment on a support if the experiment has a reactivity worth of between 0.002 and around 0.006 delta K/K.
Insert or remove experiments (samples) with a worth of less than 0.002 delta K/K with a rabbit system or by hand while the reactor is critical.
Discuss the methods of handling experiments and examine the process for consistency with the licensee’s administrative controls and procedures.

Evaluate how the licensee assures that experiments do not cause reactivity anomalies, considering operator control over rabbit insertions/removals, experiments shadowing nuclear detectors, and experiments interfering with control rod/blade movement.
Verify if the licensee has considered the use of engineered safety features (such as remote handling devices, time, distance, and shielding) in the planning and conduct of experiments.

preconditions.text: The facilities and materials being inspected must be licensed under 10 CFR Part 50 (not governed by other types of licenses).
The documentation is already available at the office.
Reviewing procedures prior to onsite inspection is practical.
If direct observation is not practical, review records and discuss with cognizant personnel instead.
The irradiation of explosives is authorized
If a regulating rod is changed by the conservative cut-off amount
Transferring byproduct material produced or used under the reactor license to the holder of a materials license.
The expected radiation field for the personnel does not present an exposure problem.
When an experiment is being removed from the reactor
Removal of an experiment from the reactor cannot be directly observed.

constraints.text: Reference guidance (such as ANSI/ANS Standards or Division 2 Regulatory Guides) is for technical evaluation only and must not be used as requirements unless the licensee has committed in writing to use them.
Recommended sample sizes are for broad planning purposes and typical depth, and are not rigid requirements.
Do not review experiments or changes to experiments that have been previously reviewed by NRC personnel.
The failure of experiments containing special nuclear material or explosives may result in a significant radioactive release and may be the maximum hypothetical accident at some reactors.
Limits for the explosives that NRR has approved can be found in the TS
If the capsule has ruptured, the opening of the experiment tube could be radiologically hazardous or cause a reactivity transient.
Experiments must contain guidance or cautions for the operator and experimenter, including those established by the safety review committee.
Operators and experimenters must understand the hazards associated with an experiment and the methods to identify and respond to them.
The operator must be able to detect any large change in reactivity during an experiment.
Requirements for the control of irradiated materials are specified in 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee's procedures for the control of radioactive materials.
The transfer of byproduct material must be done in accordance with 10 CFR 30.41.
Inadequate physical constraints can cause an experiment in a pool-type reactor to escape and float to the surface, causing potential reactivity and exposure problems.
Experiments must be fixed in place such that a simple movement cannot change the position of the experiment.
Experiments that are of negative reactivity on insertion into the reactor core must meet TS requirements.
Experimental activities are required to be consistent with the licensee’s approved administrative controls and procedures for experiments.
Experiments are assumed to be contaminated and highly radioactive until proven otherwise.
Apparatus and mechanisms other than controls, the operation of which may affect reactivity or power level, must be manipulated only with the knowledge and consent of a licensed operator or SRO.
Access to areas where radiation is present must be posted in accordance with 10 CFR Parts 20.1902 and 20.1903 and limited as required by the licensee’s radiation protection program.
